channel.title: Ai on Sujith Quintelier
channel.description: Recent content in Ai on Sujith Quintelier

item.title: security: How to better protect your growing business in an AI-powered world
item.description:
Microsoft Security Blog says growing businesses should use built-in security to help keep operations running, protect customer trust, and support growth in an AI-driven environment.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: security: Defense at AI speed: Microsoft’s new multi-model agentic security system tops leading industry benchmark
item.description:
Microsoft announced MDASH, a new multi-model agentic scanning harness for AI-powered cyber defense. The post says it topped a leading industry benchmark, but provides no technical details in the excerpt.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: dotnet: Building an AI-Powered Conference App with .NET’s Composable AI Stack
item.description:
The post describes building ConferencePulse, an AI-powered conference assistant, using .NET’s composable AI stack. It highlights Microsoft.Extensions.AI, DataIngestion, VectorData, Model Context Protocol, and Microsoft Agent Framework as the main components.

  • Source: .NET

item.title: security: AI-powered defense for an AI-accelerated threat landscape
item.description:
Microsoft says it is partnering with Anthropic and other industry players to apply leading AI models, along with its platforms and expertise, to convert AI-driven threat discovery into scalable protection.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: security: Incident response for AI: Same fire, different fuel
item.description:
Microsoft published a blog post on incident response for AI, focusing on how existing IR practices still apply while AI-specific telemetry, tools, and skills are needed. It frames AI incidents as similar to traditional response work but with different signals and operational needs.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: azure: [Launched] Generally Available: Azure Red Hat OpenShift adds NVIDIA H100 and H200 GPU support
item.description:
Azure Red Hat OpenShift (ARO) now supports NVIDIA H100 and H200 GPU-based Azure VM SKUs. The update targets large-scale AI, machine learning, and HPC workloads on the managed OpenShift service.

  • Source: Azure Updates

item.title: security: Inside an AI‑enabled device code phishing campaign
item.description:
Microsoft describes a new device code phishing campaign that uses AI and end-to-end automation to generate live authentication codes on demand. The result is more scalable account compromise, higher success rates, and longer post-compromise access.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: github: Copilot SDK in public preview
item.description:
GitHub Copilot SDK is now in public preview. It provides building blocks to embed Copilot’s agentic capabilities into applications, workflows, and platform services.

  • Source: GitHub

item.title: github: Research, plan, and code with Copilot cloud agent
item.description:
GitHub says Copilot cloud agent, formerly Copilot coding agent, is no longer limited to pull-request workflows. This expands how it can be used for research, planning, and coding tasks.

  • Source: GitHub

item.title: dotnet: Generative AI for Beginners .NET: Version 2 on .NET 10
item.description:
Microsoft announced Version 2 of Generative AI for Beginners .NET, a free course rebuilt for .NET 10. It adds Microsoft.Extensions.AI, updated RAG patterns, and new agent framework content across five lessons focused on production-ready AI apps.

  • Source: .NET

item.title: k8s: Running Agents on Kubernetes with Agent Sandbox
item.description:
Kubernetes SIG Apps is developing Agent Sandbox, a new API for singleton, stateful AI agent workloads that need persistent identity, isolation, and suspend/resume behavior. The project adds a Sandbox CRD plus extensions like warm pools to reduce cold starts and support untrusted code execution.

  • Source: Kubernetes Official Blog

item.title: security: Secure agentic AI end-to-end
item.description:
Microsoft announced new security capabilities for the agentic AI era at RSAC 2026, focused on securing agents, securing AI foundations, and using agents alongside experts for defense. The post frames these as purpose-built additions to cover the AI estate end to end.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: security: New tools and guidance: Announcing Zero Trust for AI
item.description:
Microsoft added a new AI pillar to its Zero Trust materials, including an updated workshop, reference architecture, guidance, and a new assessment tool. The announcement focuses on applying Zero Trust principles to AI systems.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: security: Observability for AI Systems: Strengthening visibility for proactive risk detection
item.description:
The post says observability is becoming necessary as AI systems become more autonomous, because visibility into AI behavior can help detect risk earlier and support secure development. It is presented as a Microsoft Security Blog article focused on proactive risk detection.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog

item.title: azure: [Launched] Generally Available: Azure SRE Agent with new capabilities
item.description:
Azure SRE Agent is now generally available. The GA release adds new capabilities focused on incident diagnosis, automated response workflows, and improving uptime while reducing operational toil.

  • Source: Azure Updates

item.title: github: Enterprise AI Controls & agent control plane now generally available
item.description:
GitHub announced general availability of Enterprise AI Controls and the agent control plane for GitHub Enterprise administrators.

  • Source: GitHub

item.title: dotnet: Vector Data in .NET – Building Blocks for AI Part 2
item.description:
Microsoft describes Microsoft.Extensions.VectorData as a .NET library that standardizes access to vector databases for semantic search scenarios.

  • Source: .NET

item.title: security: Threat modeling AI applications
item.description:
Microsoft Security Blog notes that AI threat modeling is used to identify misuse, emergent risks, and failure modes in probabilistic and agentic AI systems.

  • Source: Microsoft Security Blog
